concepts.title: Distribution of Oceans
concepts.description: The five oceans are Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Arctic, and Southern. The Pacific Ocean covers the largest area, followed by the Atlantic and Indian Oceans. The Southern Ocean surrounds Antarctica, while the Arctic Ocean is the smallest and coldest of the five.

concepts.title: Continents
concepts.description: There are 7 continents: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America. These continents are separated by oceans and seas.

concepts.title: Distribution of Continents
concepts.description: The 7 continents are distributed between the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. The Southern Hemisphere contains Antarctica and parts of Australia, Africa, and South America, while the Northern Hemisphere contains the remaining continents.

concepts.title: Mnemonics for Oceans and Continents
concepts.description: To remember the 5 oceans, use the mnemonic 'PAIARS' (Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Arctic, and Southern). For the 7 continents, use 'Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America'.

concepts.title: Identifying Continents and Oceans
concepts.description: Students often confuse the terms 'continent' and 'country'. Continents are large landmasses, while countries are smaller political units within continents. Make sure to identify the correct classification of a geographical feature as a continent or an ocean.

concepts.title: Ocean Areas
concepts.description: The approximate areas of the 5 oceans are: Pacific (155.6 million km²), Atlantic (85.1 million km²), Indian (73.5 million km²), Arctic (14.0 million km²), and Southern (20.3 million km²).

concepts.title: Ocean Depths
concepts.description: The deepest ocean is the Pacific, with a maximum depth of approximately 11,022 meters (the Mariana Trench). The Arctic Ocean is the shallowest, with a maximum depth of approximately 5,669 meters.

concepts.title: Continental Movement
concepts.description: The continents have moved significantly over geological time scales, leading to the formation of new oceans and the breaking up of old ones. This movement is still ongoing, with the continents slowly drifting apart.
